coredata.dc:title: Solar driven photocatalytic hydrogen evolution using graphitic-carbon nitride/NSGQDs heterostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Samanta, Soumadri
Battula, Venugopala Rao
Sardana, Neha
Kailasam, Kamalakannan
coredata.dc:description: The evolution of hydrogen by splitting water and sunlight renders a promising approach to produce scalable and sustainable carbon-free green energy. In this aspect, development of semiconductor heterostructure photocatalyst can spatially separate photogenerated electron-hole at the heterojunction interface and inhibit charge recombination to synergistically enhance photocatalytic performance. Herein, for the first time, we have designed exfoliated graphitic-carbon nitride (g-CN)/N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSGQDs) heterostructure grown by one pot pyrolysis process. NSGQDs create strong π-π interactions with g-CN to construct an effective heterostructure and thus act as a efficient photosensitiser. We have made extensive investigations to understand the nature of g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure, like optical and electrochemical properties and its shows improved charge transfer kinetics with reduced recombination. As a result, g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure has achieved significantly high hydrogen evolution rate (HER) of 5.24 mmol h−1 g−1 under sunlight and with the apparent quantum yield (AQY) of 23.2%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride
Photosensitization
NSGQDs
Solar photocatalysis
H2 evolution

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure preparation and pictures of various samples with different wt% of NSGQDs loading on g-CN.
(a) PXRD of NSGQDs, g-CN and g-CN/NSGQDs, (b) TEM and (c) HRTEM image of g-CN/NSGQD3.
(a) XPS Survey spectrum; (b) High resolution S 2p XPS spectra of g-CN/NSGQD3.
(a) DR UV–Vis spectra and (b) PL spectra of NSGQDs, g-CN and g-CN/NSGQDs.
(a) Time-resolved transient decay curves of g-CN and g-CN/NSGQD3 and (b) EIS Nyquist plot NSGQDs, g-CN and g-CN/NSGQD3.

(a) Comparison of HER of (i) g-CN, (ii) g-CN/NSGQD1, (iii) g-CN/NSGQD2, (iv) g-CN/NSGQD3, (v) g-CN/NSGQD4, and (vi) g-CN/NSGQD5; (b) Stability cycle of H2 evolution using g-CN/NSGQD3. Reaction conditions: 10 mg of the photocatalyst, 4 h reaction time, 27 ml DI, 3 ml TEOS as sacrificial agent, 3 wt% Pt as co-catalyst, and Xe-lamp with visible bandpass filter as light source.
Mott-Schottky plot of (a) g-CN, (b) NSGQDs, and (c) Band energy diagram.
Diagram on the proposed charge transfer mechanism in the g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Solar driven photocatalytic hydrogen evolution using graphitic-carbon nitride/NSGQDs heterostructures

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Functional Nanomaterials, Energy and Environment Unit, Institute of Nano Science and Technology (INST), Knowledge City, Sector 81, Manauli, SAS Nagar, 140306 Mohali, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Technology (INST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Functional Nanomaterials, Energy and Environment Unit, Institute of Nano Science and Technology (INST), Knowledge City, Sector 81, Manauli, SAS Nagar, 140306 Mohali, Punjab, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Current address: Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Room 316, Satish Dhawan Block, Indian Institute of Technology Ropar, Ropar, Punjab, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: NSGQDs photosensitizer deposition over the g-CN surface (g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure) charges distribution and improve the electron transfer kinetics and utilize solar light to enhance hydrogen evolution rate through photocatalytic water splitting.

The evolution of hydrogen by splitting water and sunlight renders a promising approach to produce scalable and sustainable carbon-free green energy. In this aspect, development of semiconductor heterostructure photocatalyst can spatially separate photogenerated electron-hole at the heterojunction interface and inhibit charge recombination to synergistically enhance photocatalytic performance. Herein, for the first time, we have designed exfoliated graphitic-carbon nitride (g-CN)/N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSGQDs) heterostructure grown by one pot pyrolysis process. NSGQDs create strong π-π interactions with g-CN to construct an effective heterostructure and thus act as a efficient photosensitiser. We have made extensive investigations to understand the nature of g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure, like optical and electrochemical properties and its shows improved charge transfer kinetics with reduced recombination. As a result, g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure has achieved significantly high hydrogen evolution rate (HER) of 5.24 mmol h−1 g−1 under sunlight and with the apparent quantum yield (AQY) of 23.2%.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen is a potential candidate for the future replacement of fossil fuels, due to its high energy density and water as the only by-product. Photocatalytic water splitting to convert solar-to-hydrogen (H2) is considered to be a significant and extraordinary pathway for production of H2 for future global energy crisis and environment issues [1–4]. Fujishima and Honda are the pioneer where they demonstrated photoelectrochemical water splitting using a n-type TiO2 electrode to produce hydrogen [5]. Since then, many semiconductors have been explored with great interest as photocatalyst [6–9]. However, hydrogen evolution efficiency of the photocatalyst is still far from the threshold of the desire performance due to high charge recombination, low absorption in the visible range, and low mobility of photogenerated charges [10–13].

Numerous efforts have been devoted for developing an efficient photocatalyst. Recently, some two-dimensional (2D) semiconductors have been extensively investigated owing to their unique electronic, mechanical and optical properties [4,11,14]. It has been reported that these 2D semiconductors are potentially efficient photocatalysts for photocatalytic water splitting, significantly superior to conventional photocatalysts. These 2D semiconductor could be promising photocatalyst due to the short distance for the photogenerated electron and hole migration, improving the possibility of efficient electron-hole separation [15,16]. Meanwhile, most conventional photocatalysts mainly harvest ultraviolet light, leaving visible light which makes up most of the solar spectrum. Hence, to overcome the bottleneck and for the complete utilization of sunlight, a new highly effective photocatalyst for photocatalytic water-splitting is urgently needed.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-CN, commonly known as g-C3N4) has emerged as promising metal-free visible light photocatalyst with suitable conduction band and valence band for H2 and O2 production through water splitting, respectively[17–19]. It has been considered one of the most promising photocatalyst for H2 evolution owing to its abundance, chemical stability, anti-photocorrosion, and visible light response [20–22]. Unfortunately, the photocatalytic activity of bulk g-CN for visible-light H2 evolution is quite low because of low surface area and higher recombination rate of the charge carriers. In this regard, various strategies have been suggested to increase the photocatalytic performance including the heterojunction formation, exfoliation, photosensitization, doping with metal/non-metal, composition, and formation of nanoarchitectures [23–29]. Reducing the dimensionality of bulk g-CN into 2D sheets has increased specific surface area, active sites and improved the charge separation [30,31]. These are some of the beneficial steps employed towards the improvement of photocatalytic H2 evolution.

Using liquid phase exfoliation process, bulk g-CN could be exfoliated into sheet-like structure. It has been theoretically and experimentally shown that bulk g-CN could be exfoliated into sheet-like structure by IPA or water, lead to enhancement in photocatalytic H2 evolution due to increase of surface area especially surface-active edges. For example, Zhang et al. reported the surface energy of g-C3N4 is about 115 mJ/m2 which matches well with the water surface energy (102 mJ/m2) [32]. Qing et al. exfoliated bulk g-C3N4 into atomically thin mesoporous nanomesh using liquid-exfoliation technique [33]. Since, exfoliated g-CN is less explored as photocatalyst, the studies to improve its solar-driven photocatalytic activity are worth and warranted for arising energy needs. We have foreseen that photosensitization could improve the charge carrier concentration over exfoliated g-CN, which increase the carrier mobility and decrease the recombination, which will be adventitious to improve the solar-driven photocatalytic H2 evolution.

Photosensitizer will increase the photon harvesting range as well as the density of photogenerated electron on the conduction band of the photocatalyst with large band gaps and thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst. But photosensitizer molecules exhibit fast photo-generated charge carrier recombination which inhibits H2 evolution. Fast charge carrier recombination could be slowed down if these photo-generated electrons could be transferred to the low-lying conduction band of another semiconductor. Congruously, Yu et al. reported carbon quantum dots as a photosensitizer to enhance photocatalytic hydrogen evolution of TiO2 [34]. Ludwig Bartels and group synthesized g-C3N4 coupled with CdS quantum dots for efficient visible light photocatalytic hydrogen generation [35]. In recent years, N, S-graphene quantum dots (NSGQDs) have been extensively explored for many photocatalytic applications due to their unique features such as low toxicity, eco-friendly, chemically inert, stability, good conductivity, cost efficient, strong and tuneable optical absorption and emission properties that can greatly affect the photocatalytic activity [36]. In this connection, some of the notable reports are briefed, like Xie et al. reported S, N co-doped GQD/TiO2 composite for efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution [37]. Yuanyuan Lin and group synthesized S, N co-doped GQDs decorated CdSe with improved photoelectric properties [38]. N-doped graphene quantum dot and plasmonic Ag-sensitized ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride nanosheet were designed for enhanced photocatalytic application [39]. Sutapa Ghosh and group synthesized GQDs decorated TiO2 and established the role of GQDs as a photosensitizer for enhanced hydrogen generation [40]. Lie et reported strongly coupled CdS/GQDs for highly efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [41]. It was proposed that prepared GQDs act as a photosensitizer to enhance photocatalytic activity. Sharma et al. have reported S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots, synthesized by annealing process, with enhanced optical properties [42].

Moreover, one pot synthesis is the better way for the construction of effective heterojunction formation, which improves the better interface contact and interaction between the semiconductors. This will enhance the better charge separation, facile charge transfer efficiency and as well as the photocatalytic H2 evolution [43]. NSGQDs rich in amino groups could provide ideal site for the interfacial interaction with the surface amino group rich g-CN, resulting in the formation of effective g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure [44]. Moreover, the recent advancement to enhance the visible light assisted H2 generation rate of photocatalysts prompted us to study the effect of photosensitization towards the H2 evolution efficiency of organic semiconductor materials. Hence, sensitization of g-CN appears to be a promising strategy for harvesting solar energy by improving photo-generated charge separation, enhancing visible light absorption and in turn the photocatalytic activity. To the best of our knowledge, there are no report on one-pot synthesized NSGQDs sensitized exfoliated g-CN photocatalyst for solar light assisted H2 evolution.

Herein, we have fabricated g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure photocatalyst for solar light assisted water splitting to generate hydrogen. One-pot pyrolysis process to produce NSGQDs accompanied by the further calcination to synthesize g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructures. Here, thiourea play dual roles, i.e., precursor for g-CN and source of S, N for the preparation of N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSGQDs). The experimental data established the role of NSGQDs as a potential photosensitizer and the formation of g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructures. Moreover, thorough characterisations were carried out by probing the optical and electrical properties which leads us to probe the photocatalytic mechanism in a plausible manner.

Analytical grade chemicals and solvents used without further purification for our experiments. The chemicals and their sources of procurement are as follows: citric acid, thiourea (TCI), triethanolamine (TEOA) (Sigma Aldrich), and sodium sulfate (Merck). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) data recorded using on a Bruker D8 advance diffractometer with a Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 Å) source operating at 40 kV and 40 mA. JEOL, JSM-6610 instrument was used to perform scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The optical properties of as photocatalysts were conducted and measured by a UV–vis spectroscopy recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer over a wavelength range of 300–800 nm with the resolution of 1 nm. XPS spectra were recorded on a Thermofisher scientific (Model: Nexsa) apparatus equipped with a dual anode X-ray source. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a Fluorolog HORIBA instrument at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. To quantify the hydrogen evolution rate, Shimadzu GC-2014 instrument were used.

Analytical grade chemicals and solvents used without further purification for our experiments. Herein, we prepared ultrathin g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructures via thermal polymerization of thiourea and citric acid as precursor, as depicted schematically in Fig. 1 . For the preparation of exfoliated g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure, 2.5 g thiourea and a certain amount of citric acid (2.5 mg, 5.0 mg, 10.0 mg, 20.0 mg and 30.0 mg) were added and mixed well using mortar and pestle. Then the mixture was kept in crucible with a cover and heated to 300 °C for 15 min and then further heated to 550 °C for 3 h with a ramp rate of 5 °C/min. Then the product was collected and probe sonicated for 2 h, using water as solvent. Depending on wt% of NSGQDs loading on g-CN, the samples were labelled as g-CN/NSGQD1, g-CN/NSGQD2, g-CN/NSGQD3, g-CN/NSGQD4, and g-CN/NSGQD5 with 1.2, 2.5, 4.8, 9.1 and 13.0 wt% of NSGQDs, respectively. To determine the percentage of NSGQDs in the product, first we estimated the actual yield for g-CN and NSGQDs in the separate reactions. All these five reactions were kept at same time in the same furnace to overcome the effect of temperature, heating time and ramping rate effect. Details of the samples are given in Table S1.

Electrochemical measurements were performed in a standard three-electrode system, whereas platinum electrode was used as a counter electrode and Ag/AgCl (in saturated KCl) electrode was taken as a reference electrode. Herein, we used 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution as the electrolyte. Electrochemical measurements were performed and recorded in Metrohm Autolab (M204 multichannel potentiostat galvanostat). We have used standard glassy carbon electrode as a working electrode. Nafion was used as a binder (2 mg photocatalyst and 20 ml of 10 wt% nafion solution) to coat the photocatalyst on the working electrode.

Photocatalytic hydrogen generation through water splitting was performed under ambient temperature and atmospheric pressure in a 50 ml round-bottom (RB) flask. A 400-watt Xe lamp (Newport 66921) with a visible band-pass filter was used as an irradiation light source for our photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction. RB was positioned at a distance of 10 cm from the light source. The focused intensity of the Xe lamp on the flask was about 73 mW cm−2. Natural sunlight hydrogen evolution reaction was measured in the month of 9th September 2020 at 11:00, 12:00, 13:00 and 14:00 h with the solar radiation of 95, 81, 82 and 55 mW cm−2, respectively. Ray virtual radiation actinometer (Newport, Model 91150 V) was used to measure the incident light intensity. In a typical experiment, we took mixed solvent containing 27 ml DI water and 3 ml TEOA (as hole/proton scavenger agent). In that mixed solvent, 10 mg of the photocatalyst was added and dispersed well. In all our photocatalytic experiment, we used 3 wt% Pt as co-catalyst which was prepared by in-situ photo-deposition method. Finally, the photocatalytic reaction setup was vented using a vacuum pump and then, nitrogen gas was purged for 30 min. During the photocatalytic reaction process, the reaction setup was kept under constant stirring.

The crystalline structure of the photocatalysts was probed using powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) as shown in Fig. 2 a. For g-CN and g-CN/NSGQDs samples, PXRD pattern present dominant distinctive peak at 27.4° corresponds to the (002) graphite interlayer reflection and the less intense peak at 13.2° corresponds to (100) in-plane repeat heptazine units of g-CN. For NSGQDs, a very broad peak at 27.1° corresponds to graphite (002) crystal phase has been observed, reflecting that as prepared NSGQDs have only few layers. A low intense peak at 41.2° corresponding to (100) lattice plane also observed. g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure shown no shift in the peak at 27.4° (corresponds to g-CN (002) lattice plane), which indicates that the stacking of heptazine sheets in g-CN remain intact during the formation of heterojunction of g-CN and NSGQDs. Furthermore, a small intensity peak at 41.2° correspond to NSGQDs (100) lattice plane is clearly visible in the as prepared g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructured photocatalysts. This confirms the presence of NSGQDs in the as prepared g-CN/NSGQDs photocatalysts.

The morphological structure of the as prepared photocatalysts was characterized by TEM and HRTEM, as shown in Fig. 1b-c. TEM image in Fig. 2b reveals the as prepared g-CN/NSGQDs possess a two-dimensional thin sheet-like structure. However, it is very interesting to note from Fig. 2b that the NSGQDs with the average size of approximately 6.8 nm were successfully in-situ synthesized and deposited on the surface of g-CN uniformly during the annealing process. The HRTEM image shown in Fig. 2c having 0.246 nm interfringe distance correspond to (100) lattice plane of NSGQDs. The TEM and HRTEM images g-CN/NSGQDs confirms that the NSGQDs are uniformly deposited on the surface of g-CN. SEM elemental mapping also carried out to survey the decoration of NSGQDs on the g-CN surface (Fig. S1). The elemental mapping images confirms the uniform deposition of NSGQDs over the g-CN surface. The existence of S-atoms in the g-CN/NSGQDs confirms the formation of NSGQDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed to get the insight into the surface nature of g-CN, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The XPS survey spectrum of g-CN/NSGQD3 is shown in Fig. 3a, confirming the existence of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and S 2p at the binding energy value of 288.1 eV, 399.8 eV, 533.1 eV and 163.5 eV, respectively. Three deconvoluted peaks of high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. S2) with binding energy of 288.1 eV, 286.5 eV and 284.8 eV correspond to N-C = N, N-(C)3, and C-C bonds, respectively.[45] High resolution N 1 s XPS spectra can be deconvoluted into four peaks with binding energy of 399.2 eV, 400.6 eV, 401.5 eV and 404.9 eV, which corresponds to C-N = C, N-(C)3, C-N-H and N-Oxide bonds, respectively.[45] As shown in Fig. 3b, the deconvolution of S 2p renders four peaks at 162.6, 164.2, 165.5 and 168.5 eV. Two main peaks at 162.6 and 164.2, corresponds to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2. And two peaks at 165.5 and 168.5 eV are consistent with –C–S(O)2–C– sulfone bridges. [46] This signifies the existence of NSGQDs over the surface of g-CN.

Fig. 4 a shows the DR UV–Vis spectra of the as prepared photocatalysts. Pristine g-CN exhibited a steep absorption edge of around 433 nm and NSGQDs has a broader absorption until 720 nm. It was found that g-CN/NSGQDs exhibited a blue shift in the band-edge absorption of g-CN photocatalyst, and a gradual shift to the red edge of the absorption band in the visible region with the increase in percentage of NSGQDs content. This indicates that the g-CN/NSGQDs has a significant improved light-harvesting capability. The red shift of the absorption band might be due to the broad absorption of NSGQDs and this reveals a strong interfacial interaction between g-CN and NSGQDs in the g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure photocatalyst. Band gap energy (Eg) of different samples was also calculated using Tauc plot (Fig. S3). The calculated Eg of g-CN and NSGQDs are 2.75 eV and 1.65 eV, respectively.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy provides important information of photogenerated charge separation and recombination at the interface of the heterostructured photocatalyst. Fig. 4b shows the PL profiles of g-CN, NSGQDs, and g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructures. g-CN exhibit a peak around 454 nm which is attributed to the band-to-band PL transition. Here in the PL profile graph the g-CN shows highest intensity which implies the lower photocatalytic hydrogen evolution efficiency due to low photogenerated charge separation efficiency. The PL spectra of all the g-CN/NSGQDs photocatalysts shows relatively lower intensity, which proves that the photogenerated charge recombination process in these samples is slower compared to the g-CN. Especially, the g-CN/NSGQD3 photocatalyst presume better charge separation which leads to higher photocatalytic activity. Here, NSGQDs act as photosensitizer that efficiently absorbs visible light and thereby generate sufficient photogenerated electron which is transferred to the g-CN surface, resulting in the lowering of the photogenerated charge recombination. This in turn reflects in the quenching of the PL intensity which leads us to anticipate enhanced photocatalytic activity for the g-CN/NSGQDs. [47]

Further to recheck the photogenerated charge separation efficiency of the heterostructure photocatalysts, the time-resolved PL decay spectra was also performed for g-CN/NSGQDs, as shown in Fig. 5 a. Although there is a decrease in average fluorescence lifetime of g-CN/NSGQD3 (τav = 2.88 ns) compared with g-CN (τav = 3.42 ns), but the non-radiative decay time increased from 4.69 ns (g-CN) to 5.82 ns (g-CN/NSGQD3), as tabulated in Table S2. This increased non-radiative decay lifetime usually implies more efficient photogenerated charge separation [34,35]. Thus, the recombination of electron and hole is inhibited by the designing of g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements extensively probed the catalytic charge transfer kinetics of the samples through Nyquist plot as shown in Fig. 5b. The semicircle arc radius in the Nyquist plot reflects the facial charge transfer resistance (Ra) across the catalyst interface. The decrease in semicircle arc radius of g-CN/NSGQD3 compared to g-CN indicates the decrease in facial charge transfer resistance which implies improved charge transfer efficiency on the catalyst interface of NSGQDs and g-CN. EIS study further confirm that the heterostructure formation facilitate a strong interaction between g-CN and the NSGQDs interface rather than a simple physical mixture, which is mainly responsible for better facial charge migration and leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance.

The photocatalytic H2 evolution performance of the g-CN/NSGQDs photocatalyst were carried under visible light irradiation (Xe-lamp with visible bandpass filter) for 4 h by using triethanolamine (TEOS) as the sacrificial agent and Pt as co-catalyst (shown in Fig. 6 a). NSGQDs didn’t produce any H2 under the above-mentioned condition. It can be seen that pristine g-CN photocatalyst shows hydrogen evolution rate (HER) of 0.96 mmol h−1 g−1. Compared to pristine g-CN, all other photocatalysts showed remarkable enhanced photocatalytic activity. Photocatalytic efficiency considerably increased with the increase in NSGQDs content and achieved the highest HER value of 4.34 mmol h−1 g−1, which is 4.5 times higher compared to pristine g-CN, for the best photocatalyst g-CN/NSGQD3. However, further increase of NSGQDs content results in decrease in photocatalytic H2 generation.

Further we performed the photocatalytic H2 evolution efficiency of our best photocatalyst (g-CN/NSGQD3) under natural sunlight. The reaction was performed under natural sunlight for 4 h on 9th September 2020 from 10:00 to 14:00 h. As expected, g-CN/NSGQD3 produced significantly very high value of hydrogen of about 5.24 mmol h−1 g−1. It was observed that photocatalytic H2 evolution efficiency of g-CN/NSGQD3 is higher in natural sunlight compared under artificial visible light simulator (Xe-lamp with visible bandpass filter). Thus, from the above results, it is clear that the as prepared g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure is highly efficient for natural sunlight H2 production. To understand the effectiveness of NSGQDs to improve the photocatalytic H2 production efficiency, we have evaluated the apparent quantum yield (AQY) for the best photocatalyst g-CN/NSGQD3 under Xe-lamp using the formula
A Q Y
%
=
numberofhydrogenmoluculeevolved × 2
numberofincidentphoton
× 100 %
Remarkably, very high AQY of about 23.2% was attained at 400 nm.

It is instructive to know which wavelength has more impact on the hydrogen evolution reaction. To attain this wavelength, wavelength dependent hydrogen generation was measured for the best catalyst g-CN/NSGQD3 using monochromatic light. As shown in Fig. S4, highest HER was obtained at a wavelength of 400 nm. HER contribution at different wavelength is tabulated at Table S3. It was seen that at higher wavelength HER contribution decreased and beyond 650 nm, no hydrogen was detected in GC. Although the main contribution to the HER comes from the lower visible region, higher visible range also contributes in HER. This indicates that the deposition of SNGQDs on g-CN have great contribution to hydrogen evolution in the visible range.

To check the robustness of the photocatalyst, the recycling experiment was carried out using g-CN/NSGQD3 as shown in Fig. 6b. The photocatalytic activity of g-CN/NSGQD3 shows no obvious H2 generation rate decay in 4 cycle tests for 16 h, thereby proving the excellent photocatalytic stability of the as prepared g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructures for visible light-driven H2 evolution.

The Mott-Schottky of g-CN and NSGQDs were performed under dark conditions at an applied frequency of 1000 Hz to determine the band edge positions and further understand the plausible charge transfer mechanism, as shown in Fig. 7 a-b. The Mott-Schottky plot of the photocatalysts exhibits positive slopes, which is a typical characteristic of n-type semiconductor. The flat-band potential (Vfb) of g-CN and NSGQDs were determined to be −1.34 V and −1.95 V (vs Ag/AgCl), respectively. The flat potential (Vfb) for most of the n-type semiconductors lies 0.1 V below the CBM (conduction band minima).[48] Thus, the CBM calculated from the Vfb for g-CN and NSGQDs are of −1.44 V and −2.05 V (vs Ag/AgCl), respectively. Further Eg was used further to locate the valence band maxima (VBM) as 1.31 V and −0.40 V (vs Ag/AgCl) for g-CN and NSGQDs, respectively. The band energy diagram with respect to NHE is provided in Fig. 7c which clearly demonstrate a type II band alignment between the g-CN and NSGQDs. The photogenerated electron can easily move from the CBM of NSGQDs to the CBM of g-CN due to the type II heterojunction structure. This band arrangement favours more electron injection into the g-CN, causing population of electron in conduction band of g-CN, which may enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of g-CN for the visible light hydrogen evolution.

We interpret the mechanistic insight into the enhanced visible light photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of the as prepared g-CN/NSGQDs, as shown in Fig. 8 . Thus, with the increment of NSGQDs on g-CN, photocatalytic activity gradually increased and reached the highest HER (4.34 mmol h−1 g−1) for g-CN/NSGQD3. This is due to the strong absorption ability of NSGQDs in the far visible range and improved charged separation combined with the synergistic effect of both the system as postscripted in the band-energy diagram. Moreover, g-CN and NSGQDs are structurally similar enabling strong π-π interaction, thus results in efficient heterojunction formation. With increasing wt% of NSGQDs, the amount of photogenerated electron from the CBM of NSGQDs to the CBM of g-CN eventually increase and populate the conduction band of g-CN. This in turn results in transfer of electrons from g-CN to the Pt co-catalyst which is responsible for the dramatic increase in H2 evolution performance. Meanwhile, further increase in the wt% of NSGQDs results in enlargement and aggregation of NSGQDs, thus, hindering the charge separation at the interface of g-CN/NSGQDs [34]. Accordingly, this results in decrement of the photocatalytic activity.

In summary, exfoliated g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure has been constructed by a one-step pyrolysis process, by which S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots sensitized g-CN were synthesized successfully. It was found that the well-defined g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure can be utilized as excellent photocatalyst for solar light driven water splitting resulting in enhanced H2 evolution due to better charge separation and higher interfacial charge migration rate along with the wide absorption in the visible region. The heterostructured photocatalyst under Xe-lamp (using visible band-pass filter) provided the highest hydrogen evolution rate of 4.34 mmol h−1 g−1, which is 4.5 times higher compared to pristine g-CN and very high AQY of 23.2% is achieved. Furthermore, with the irradiation of solar light, remarkable amount of H2 evolution is achieved, which is about 5.24 mmol h−1 g−1. It is anticipated that this current work could provide new insights of one-step procedure for the synthesis of exfoliated g-CN/NSGQDs heterostructure for highly efficient solar-light driven photocatalytic applications and provoke the possibility of large scale H2 production in natural sunlight in particular.

Soumadri Samanta: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Venugopala Rao Battula: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Neha Sardana: . Kamalakannan Kailasam: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Investigation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Soumadri Samanta thanks INST Mohali for financial support. Dr. K. Kailasam thank DST- Nano Mission NATDP funded Technology Project, File No. SR/NM/NT-06/2016 for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Details of loading of different wt% of NSGQDs on g-CN, C, N and S elemental mapping, High resolution C 1 s XPS spectra, Time-resolved transient decay curves, fluorescence lifetime.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.150409.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MgO/g-C3N4 nanocomposites as efficient water splitting photocatalysts under visible light irradiation
